one_line_takeaway: By decoupling safety-rule retrieval from reasoning via an agentic tool-use framework (ASCL) and correcting RL's bias toward over-using that retrieval tool via inverse-frequency advantage reweighting (IFPO), the paper achieves a better empirical safety/over-refusal Pareto front on Qwen3 models than context-distillation baselines, but explicitly does not show the approach improves (and sometimes slightly hurts) general reasoning performance.
key_finding: On Table 1 (Qwen3-4B/8B/14B), the full pipeline (BC then IFPO) achieves the best joint safety/over-refusal averages, e.g. for Qwen3-4B: IFPO reaches 98.87% safety average vs. 65.66% (Pure), 77.85% (Short), 92.78% (ASCL-ZS), 90.23% (STAR-1), 83.10% (STAR-1-mix), while simultaneously achieving the lowest over-refusal average of 6.00% among trained variants (vs. STAR-1's 22.03% and STAR-1-mix's 18.18%); the BC stage alone beats STAR-1 by 'over 5%' in safety averages across all three model sizes. On general reasoning (Table 2: MATH-500, GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, GSM8K, ARC-Challenge), the authors explicitly state they do NOT observe consistent gains from the safety-tuned models, and IFPO's harmonic-average (H-avg.) improvement over plain accuracy is driven largely by better safety/over-refusal balance rather than raw reasoning accuracy (e.g., 14B IFPO drops to 92.46% on MATH-500 avg@16 vs. ASCL-ZS's 96.75%). Ablation (Table 3, Qwen3-4B): IFPO (Safety 98.87, O.R. 6.00, Reasoning 79.06, H-avg 89.82) outperforms plain GRPO (99.08, 4.42, 76.49, 89.21) and a hand-tuned reward-penalty variant 'GRPO w/ penalty' (98.71, 5.51, 74.51, 87.89), which the authors show collapses rule-consultation behavior almost entirely within 50 training steps rather than learning selective retrieval.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Adaptive Safe Context Learning (ASCL), a framework that reformulates LLM safety alignment as a multi-turn tool-use (ReAct/MCP-style) process in which the model autonomously decides when to retrieve external safety-rule documents (107 safety terms across 21 categories) rather than having safety rules baked into its CoT training data via context distillation. Building on ASCL, the authors introduce a post-training pipeline (behavior cloning on curated tool-use trajectories, then RL) and a new RL algorithm, Inverse Frequency Policy Optimization (IFPO), which reweights GRPO advantage estimates within a rollout group by the inverse frequency of each sample's action type (tool-retrieval vs. direct response) to counteract an empirically observed RL-induced bias toward over-consulting the safety-rule tool.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the classic security-paper sense; the paper's concern is a dual-failure mode of LLM safety alignment: (1) LLMs failing to refuse genuinely harmful/jailbreak-style requests (measured via WildJailbreak, JBB-Behaviours, WildChat, StrongReject, WildGuardTest), and (2) LLMs over-refusing superficially sensitive but benign requests (measured via XSTest, OKTest, OR-Bench-Hard, plus the benign/sensitive subsets of WildJailbreak and WildGuardTest). The 'threat' addressed is thus alignment miscalibration (both under- and over-refusal), not an external attacker crafting novel exploits.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation (Sec 5.3 heading verbatim: 'Generalization to general reasoning tasks is not demonstrated') — the safety post-training does not reliably transfer to or preserve general reasoning capability, with some model sizes (e.g., 14B on MATH-500) showing decreases. All benchmark evaluations were run only once (not averaged over multiple seeds) except MATH-500 (avg@16) and GSM8K (avg@8); other evaluations (JBB, WildChat, XSTest, etc.) are single-run point estimates. The safety/compliance judge is Qwen3-Guard-8B throughout (both for training reward and evaluation), creating potential judge-dependent bias not cross-validated against an independent judge. The underlying safety-rule document (107 terms/21 categories) was self-curated by the authors, and its completeness/quality is not independently validated. A known implementation quirk is flagged by the authors themselves (footnote 2): Qwen-agent's context handling patches retrieved content in place of the end-of-turn token, causing context inconsistency between the two training iterations for retrieval samples — a workaround detail rather than a fully resolved issue.

datasets_used: Salad-Bench (harmful-query training source, BC and RL)
quantitative_results: Qwen3-4B IFPO: Safety avg 98.87%, Over-refusal avg 6.00% (Table 1)
Qwen3-8B IFPO: Safety avg 98.98%, Over-refusal avg 4.33% (Table 1)
Qwen3-14B IFPO: Safety avg 98.64%, Over-refusal avg 5.84% (Table 1)
Qwen3-4B BC alone beats STAR-1 by 'over 5%' in safety benchmarks across all three model sizes
Ablation on Qwen3-4B (Table 3): GRPO (Safety 99.08, O.R. 4.42, Reasoning 76.49, H-avg 89.21) vs GRPO w/ penalty (98.71, 5.51, 74.51, 87.89) vs IFPO (98.87, 6.00, 79.06, 89.82)
IFPO reduces rule-consultation rate by about 10 percentage points relative to plain GRPO by the end of training (Fig. 5)
General reasoning (Table 2) shows no consistent gain from safety training; e.g. Qwen3-14B IFPO MATH-500 avg@16 = 92.46% vs. ASCL-ZS's 96.75%
baselines_compared: Short (one-line safety reminder in system prompt)
Long (system prompt listing all 21 safety-category titles)
RAG (retrieved safety terms padded into prompt)
GRPO w/ reward penalty (hand-tuned 0.1 penalty on rule consultation for sensitive prompts)
